- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so m…
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep…
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/…
- claude-api: Build apps with the Claude API or Anthropic SDK.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`/`claude_agent_sdk`, or user asks to use Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Agent SDK.
DO NOT TRIGGER when: code imports `openai`/other AI SDK, ge…
- mw-cp: Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process — pre-commit checks, doc updates, detailed commit, and push
- claude-mem:do: Execute a plan using subagents for implementation
- claude-mem:make-plan: Create an implementation plan with documentation discovery
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.

content.content:
=== PLAN ===
# COR24 BASIC v1 Interpreter

BASIC interpreter written in Pascal, compiled to p-code via p24p,
running on pvm.s. Dogfoods tc24r → p24p → BASIC toolchain.

## Current State
- Steps 001-007 complete: spec, toolchain, tokens, tokenizer, program
  store, expression parser, statement handlers (PRINT, LET)
- Step 008 (REPL skeleton) in progress: monolithic basic.pas compiles
  and runs but is at the ~8KB UART input limit
- Waiting on p-code unit system (sw-cor24-pcode load-time-resolution)
  to split into independently compiled modules

## Phases
1. Spec documents and saga setup (done)
2. Toolchain validation (done — p24p has procedures, arrays, chars)
3. Tokenizer and program store (done)
4. Immediate-mode statements: PRINT, LET (done as monolith)
5. **Unit system integration** (blocked on sw-cor24-pcode)
   - Build pipeline for multi-unit compilation
   - Split monolith into unit modules
   - Remove 8KB source size constraint
6. Stored program (LIST, RUN, NEW, line editing)
7. Program runner (GOTO, IF...THEN, END, STOP)
8. Subroutines and loops (GOSUB/RETURN, FOR/NEXT)
9. Console polish, INPUT, error handling
10. Hardware demos and validation

## Key Design Decisions
- Implementation language: Pascal (compiled by p24p to p-code)
- Variables: A-Z only (26 scalar integers)
- PEEK/POKE: byte-oriented MMIO (LED D2, SW2)
- SAVE/LOAD: deferred (future MMIO/I2C tape device)
- STOP without CONT in v1
- Standalone operation (no monitor integration in v1)
- Library routines for MEMCPY/MEMSET (not VM opcodes)
- **Multi-unit build**: each module compiles to a .p24 unit,
  combined via p24-load into a single .p24m image

## Toolchain Dependencies
- sw-cor24-pcode: p-code unit system (load-time-resolution.md)
  - .unit/.export/.import/.extern directives in .spc
  - xcall instruction for cross-unit calls
  - p24-load tool to combine units
  - v2 .p24 binary format with export/import tables
- sw-cor24-pascal: p24p compiler
  - Emit .unit/.export directives (or post-process .spc)
  - Forward declarations (#7) — nice to have
  - Nested procedures (#8) — nice to have

=== STEPS ===
  [x] 001-001-spec-docs [production]: Create specification and design documents for COR24 BASIC v1. Read docs/research.txt, read sw-cor24-pcode VM design, create docs/prd.md, docs/architecture.md, docs/design.md, docs/plan.md, and README.md.
  [x] 002-002-toolchain-validation [production]: Validate that the Pascal toolchain can build the BASIC interpreter. BLOCKER: requires p24p Phase 1. Write a small Pascal test program exercising procedures, arrays, char manipulation. Build pipeline: p24p → pl24r → pa24r → .p24. Run on emulator.
  [x] 003-003-token-definitions [production]: Implement token definitions and keyword table for the BASIC tokenizer. Create src/basic_tokens.pas with token type constants and keyword string table. Create src/basic_detok.pas with detokenizer.
  [x] 004-004-tokenizer [production]: Implement the BASIC tokenizer (basic_lex). Create src/basic_lex.pas with tokenize() procedure handling keywords, integers, strings, variables, operators, delimiters, REM, and end-of-line.
  [x] 005-005-program-store [production]: Implement the program store (basic_store) for managing stored BASIC lines. Packed sorted buffer of tokenized lines with insert, delete, find, next operations and byte-level memory shifting.
  [x] 006-006-expression-parser [production]: Implement the expression parser (basic_expr) using precedence climbing. Four precedence levels for comparisons, add/sub, mul/div, unary. Also create basic_vars.pas with 26-element variable table.
  [x] 007-007-immediate-statements [production]: Implement core statement handlers for immediate-mode use: PRINT, LET, implicit LET, POKE, INPUT. Also create basic_io.pas with print_char, print_string, print_integer, print_newline, read_line, parse_integer.
  [x] 008-008-repl-skeleton [production]: Implement the REPL skeleton. Create basic_repl.pas with main loop, basic_errors.pas with error codes/messages, basic_main.pas entry point, basic_runtime.pas with interpreter state. First end-to-end milestone.
  [x] 009-009-list-new-editing [production]: Implement LIST, NEW, line editing, and BYE commands. LIST with optional range, NEW clears program, bare line number deletes line, BYE halts VM.
  [x] 010-010-run-command [production]: Implement the RUN command for stored program execution. Create basic_exec.pas with execution loop, statement dispatch, state reset. Test with simple programs.
  [x] 011-011-goto-if-end [production]: Implement GOTO, IF...THEN, END, and STOP statements. GOTO searches program area. IF evaluates condition and jumps on true. END/STOP return to REPL.
  [ ] 012-012-gosub-return [production]: Implement GOSUB and RETURN statements. GOSUB stack of 64 line pointers with push/pop. Test nested subroutines and error conditions. <-- YOU ARE HERE
  [ ] 013-013-for-next [production]: Implement FOR...TO...STEP and NEXT statements. FOR stack with parallel arrays for var, limit, step, restart. Support default STEP=1 and negative steps.
  [ ] 014-014-print-formatting [production]: Polish PRINT formatting: column tracking, comma tab stops at 14-char intervals, semicolon suppression, trailing semicolon suppresses newline, mixed string/expression output.
  [ ] 015-015-input-enhancements [production]: Enhance INPUT statement with custom prompts, ?REDO on bad input, negative number handling. Polish error messages with consistent format.
  [ ] 016-016-error-polish [production]: Final polish on startup banner, error recovery, debugger-friendly state, REM reproduction in LIST, and edge case testing.
  [ ] 017-017-hardware-demos [production]: Create hardware demo programs (hello, count, blink, uart, memdump), test harness, run validation on emulator, verify PEEK/POKE MMIO, performance check, update README.
  [ ] 018-unit-build-pipeline [production]: Refactor the build pipeline for p-code unit compilation. Update tests/toolcha...
  [ ] 019-split-monolith [production]: Split src/basic.pas monolith into separate unit source files that compile ind...
  [ ] 020-input-statement [production]: Implement the INPUT statement now that the interpreter is split into units an...
  [ ] 021-startrek-spec [production]: Write a clean-room Star Trek game specification at docs/startrek-spec.md, bas...
  [ ] 022-startrek-feature-audit [production]: Audit BASIC interpreter features required by docs/startrek-spec.md. Produce d...
  [ ] 023-startrek-impl [production]: Implement the Star Trek game at examples/startrek.bas per docs/startrek-spec....
  [ ] 024-startrek-validate [production]: Validate examples/startrek.bas end-to-end on the emulator. Run a scripted pla...

=== CURRENT STEP: 012-012-gosub-return [production] ===
Status: pending
Description: Implement GOSUB and RETURN statements. GOSUB stack of 64 line pointers with push/pop. Test nested subroutines and error conditions.

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

content.content:
#!/bin/bash
# run-basic.sh — Run a .bas file through the BASIC interpreter
# Usage: ./scripts/run-basic.sh examples/hello.bas
#
# Feeds the .bas file as UART input to the compiled interpreter.
# Uses pv24t (host-side p-code trace interpreter).
set -euo pipefail

BAS="${1:?Usage: $0 <file.bas>}"
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
PV24T="$REPO_DIR/../sw-cor24-pcode/target/release/pv24t"
P24="$REPO_DIR/build/basic.p24"

if [ ! -f "$P24" ]; then
  echo "build/basic.p24 not found — run ./scripts/build-basic.sh first" >&2
  exit 1
fi

# Feed .bas content as UART input, strip all > prompts
"$PV24T" "$P24" -i "$(cat "$BAS")" -n 10000000 2>&1 | tr -d '>' | grep -v '^$'

content.text:
# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`

If  was provided, incorporate it as context for the message.

Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

---

## Phase 5 — Push

1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`

If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.

---

## Phase 6 — Checkpoint Summary

Report a brief summary:
- ✅ Checks passed
- 📝 Docs updated (list files)
- 🔖 Commit hash and message
- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

Flag any items that were skipped or need follow-up.
```

---

## Usage
```
/mw-cp
/mw-cp adds OAuth token refresh with retry logic

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(repl): add GOSUB and RETURN statements

GOSUB pushes current exec cursor onto a 64-slot stack and jumps to
target line via store_find; RETURN pops and resumes. RUN resets gp.
New error codes: 6 (GOSUB stack overflow), 7 (RETURN without GOSUB).

To fit the 8KB UART source limit the monolith was aggressively
compacted: KW width reduced from 8 to 6 (RETURN is the longest
keyword), ks procedure and all keyword init calls trimmed to 6 char
params, unused MT constant removed, and leading indentation/blank
lines dropped throughout. Source is now 8030 bytes.

Tested: nested GOSUB with arithmetic (A=1, +1, *10 -> 20), multiple
calls to a single sub, and bare RETURN error.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push origin main 2>&1 | tail -5

message.content: how can I play the game at the CLI?

content.text:
Two ways:

**Run a .bas file** (scripted):
```
./scripts/run-basic.sh examples/hello.bas
```

**Interactive REPL** — `run-basic.sh` feeds a file as stdin, so for an interactive session run `pv24t` directly:
```
../sw-cor24-pcode/target/release/pv24t build/basic.p24
```
Then type BASIC at the `>` prompt (`10 PRINT "HI"`, `RUN`, `LIST`, `NEW`, `BYE`). Build first with `./scripts/build-basic.sh` if `build/basic.p24` is missing.

Note: there's no Star Trek game yet — that's steps 021–024 on the plan.

content.content:
=== PLAN ===
# COR24 BASIC v1 Interpreter

BASIC interpreter written in Pascal, compiled to p-code via p24p,
running on pvm.s. Dogfoods tc24r → p24p → BASIC toolchain.

## Current State
- Steps 001-007 complete: spec, toolchain, tokens, tokenizer, program
  store, expression parser, statement handlers (PRINT, LET)
- Step 008 (REPL skeleton) in progress: monolithic basic.pas compiles
  and runs but is at the ~8KB UART input limit
- Waiting on p-code unit system (sw-cor24-pcode load-time-resolution)
  to split into independently compiled modules

## Phases
1. Spec documents and saga setup (done)
2. Toolchain validation (done — p24p has procedures, arrays, chars)
3. Tokenizer and program store (done)
4. Immediate-mode statements: PRINT, LET (done as monolith)
5. **Unit system integration** (blocked on sw-cor24-pcode)
   - Build pipeline for multi-unit compilation
   - Split monolith into unit modules
   - Remove 8KB source size constraint
6. Stored program (LIST, RUN, NEW, line editing)
7. Program runner (GOTO, IF...THEN, END, STOP)
8. Subroutines and loops (GOSUB/RETURN, FOR/NEXT)
9. Console polish, INPUT, error handling
10. Hardware demos and validation

## Key Design Decisions
- Implementation language: Pascal (compiled by p24p to p-code)
- Variables: A-Z only (26 scalar integers)
- PEEK/POKE: byte-oriented MMIO (LED D2, SW2)
- SAVE/LOAD: deferred (future MMIO/I2C tape device)
- STOP without CONT in v1
- Standalone operation (no monitor integration in v1)
- Library routines for MEMCPY/MEMSET (not VM opcodes)
- **Multi-unit build**: each module compiles to a .p24 unit,
  combined via p24-load into a single .p24m image

## Toolchain Dependencies
- sw-cor24-pcode: p-code unit system (load-time-resolution.md)
  - .unit/.export/.import/.extern directives in .spc
  - xcall instruction for cross-unit calls
  - p24-load tool to combine units
  - v2 .p24 binary format with export/import tables
- sw-cor24-pascal: p24p compiler
  - Emit .unit/.export directives (or post-process .spc)
  - Forward declarations (#7) — nice to have
  - Nested procedures (#8) — nice to have

=== STEPS ===
  [x] 001-001-spec-docs [production]: Create specification and design documents for COR24 BASIC v1. Read docs/research.txt, read sw-cor24-pcode VM design, create docs/prd.md, docs/architecture.md, docs/design.md, docs/plan.md, and README.md.
  [x] 002-002-toolchain-validation [production]: Validate that the Pascal toolchain can build the BASIC interpreter. BLOCKER: requires p24p Phase 1. Write a small Pascal test program exercising procedures, arrays, char manipulation. Build pipeline: p24p → pl24r → pa24r → .p24. Run on emulator.
  [x] 003-003-token-definitions [production]: Implement token definitions and keyword table for the BASIC tokenizer. Create src/basic_tokens.pas with token type constants and keyword string table. Create src/basic_detok.pas with detokenizer.
  [x] 004-004-tokenizer [production]: Implement the BASIC tokenizer (basic_lex). Create src/basic_lex.pas with tokenize() procedure handling keywords, integers, strings, variables, operators, delimiters, REM, and end-of-line.
  [x] 005-005-program-store [production]: Implement the program store (basic_store) for managing stored BASIC lines. Packed sorted buffer of tokenized lines with insert, delete, find, next operations and byte-level memory shifting.
  [x] 006-006-expression-parser [production]: Implement the expression parser (basic_expr) using precedence climbing. Four precedence levels for comparisons, add/sub, mul/div, unary. Also create basic_vars.pas with 26-element variable table.
  [x] 007-007-immediate-statements [production]: Implement core statement handlers for immediate-mode use: PRINT, LET, implicit LET, POKE, INPUT. Also create basic_io.pas with print_char, print_string, print_integer, print_newline, read_line, parse_integer.
  [x] 008-008-repl-skeleton [production]: Implement the REPL skeleton. Create basic_repl.pas with main loop, basic_errors.pas with error codes/messages, basic_main.pas entry point, basic_runtime.pas with interpreter state. First end-to-end milestone.
  [x] 009-009-list-new-editing [production]: Implement LIST, NEW, line editing, and BYE commands. LIST with optional range, NEW clears program, bare line number deletes line, BYE halts VM.
  [x] 010-010-run-command [production]: Implement the RUN command for stored program execution. Create basic_exec.pas with execution loop, statement dispatch, state reset. Test with simple programs.
  [x] 011-011-goto-if-end [production]: Implement GOTO, IF...THEN, END, and STOP statements. GOTO searches program area. IF evaluates condition and jumps on true. END/STOP return to REPL.
  [x] 012-012-gosub-return [production]: Implement GOSUB and RETURN statements. GOSUB stack of 64 line pointers with push/pop. Test nested subroutines and error conditions.
  [ ] 013-013-for-next [production]: Implement FOR...TO...STEP and NEXT statements. FOR stack with parallel arrays for var, limit, step, restart. Support default STEP=1 and negative steps. <-- YOU ARE HERE
  [ ] 014-014-print-formatting [production]: Polish PRINT formatting: column tracking, comma tab stops at 14-char intervals, semicolon suppression, trailing semicolon suppresses newline, mixed string/expression output.
  [ ] 015-015-input-enhancements [production]: Enhance INPUT statement with custom prompts, ?REDO on bad input, negative number handling. Polish error messages with consistent format.
  [ ] 016-016-error-polish [production]: Final polish on startup banner, error recovery, debugger-friendly state, REM reproduction in LIST, and edge case testing.
  [ ] 017-017-hardware-demos [production]: Create hardware demo programs (hello, count, blink, uart, memdump), test harness, run validation on emulator, verify PEEK/POKE MMIO, performance check, update README.
  [ ] 018-unit-build-pipeline [production]: Refactor the build pipeline for p-code unit compilation. Update tests/toolcha...
  [ ] 019-split-monolith [production]: Split src/basic.pas monolith into separate unit source files that compile ind...
  [ ] 020-input-statement [production]: Implement the INPUT statement now that the interpreter is split into units an...
  [ ] 021-startrek-spec [production]: Write a clean-room Star Trek game specification at docs/startrek-spec.md, bas...
  [ ] 022-startrek-feature-audit [production]: Audit BASIC interpreter features required by docs/startrek-spec.md. Produce d...
  [ ] 023-startrek-impl [production]: Implement the Star Trek game at examples/startrek.bas per docs/startrek-spec....
  [ ] 024-startrek-validate [production]: Validate examples/startrek.bas end-to-end on the emulator. Run a scripted pla...

=== CURRENT STEP: 013-013-for-next [production] ===
Status: pending
Description: Implement FOR...TO...STEP and NEXT statements. FOR stack with parallel arrays for var, limit, step, restart. Support default STEP=1 and negative steps.

=== WHEN DONE ===

  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

- sw-cor24-pascal: p24p compiler

#!/bin/bash
# build-basic.sh — Compile the BASIC interpreter to build/basic.p24
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
EMBED_DIR="$(cd "$REPO_DIR/.." && pwd)"

P24P_BIN="$REPO_DIR/build/p24p.bin"
P24P_S="$EMBED_DIR/sw-cor24-pascal/compiler/p24p.s"
PL24R="$EMBED_DIR/sw-cor24-pcode/target/release/pl24r"
PA24R="$EMBED_DIR/sw-cor24-pcode/target/release/pa24r"
RUNTIME="$EMBED_DIR/sw-cor24-pascal/runtime/runtime.spc"
EMU="$EMBED_DIR/sw-cor24-emulator/target/release/cor24-emu"
if [ ! -x "$EMU" ]; then EMU="cor24-run"; fi

mkdir -p "$REPO_DIR/build"
TMP=$(mktemp -d)
trap "rm -rf $TMP" EXIT

# Cache pre-assembled p24p
if [ ! -f "$P24P_BIN" ] || [ "$P24P_S" -nt "$P24P_BIN" ]; then
  cor24-run --assemble "$P24P_S" "$P24P_BIN" /dev/null 2>&1 | head -1
fi

echo "=== Compiling basic.pas ==="

SPC_OUTPUT=$("$EMU" --load-binary "$P24P_BIN@0" --entry 0 --stack-kilobytes 8 \
  -u "$(cat "$REPO_DIR/src/basic.pas")"$'\x04' \
  --speed 0 -n 2000000000 2>&1 | grep -v '^\[UART')

if ! echo "$SPC_OUTPUT" | grep -q "; OK"; then
  echo "Compilation failed:" >&2
  echo "$SPC_OUTPUT" | grep "error" >&2
  exit 1
fi

echo "$SPC_OUTPUT" | sed 's/^UART output: //' | \
  sed -n '/^\.module/,/^\.endmodule/p' > "$TMP/basic.spc"
echo "  .spc: $(wc -l < "$TMP/basic.spc") lines"

"$PL24R" "$TMP/basic.spc" "$RUNTIME" -o "$TMP/linked.spc" 2>/dev/null
echo "  Linked"

# Patch read_line: replace readln(c) with single-char GETC
# p24p emits readln(c) as: call _p24p_read_int + call _p24p_read_ln
# We need: sys 2 (GETC, one char). Remove the read_ln call too.
sed -i '' '/_user_read_line/,/\.end/{
  s/call _p24p_read_int/sys 2/g
  /call _p24p_read_ln/d
}' "$TMP/linked.spc"
echo "  Patched read_line"

"$PA24R" "$TMP/linked.spc" -o "$REPO_DIR/build/basic.p24" 2>&1
echo "=== Built: build/basic.p24 ==="
[result-id: r11]

content.text:
Stopping to report a blocker before proceeding.

**Blocker on step 013 (FOR/NEXT):** p24p has a hard 8191-byte source input buffer (`INPUT_BUF_SIZE` in `sw-cor24-pascal/compiler/src/main.c:9`, baked into `p24p.s:11438`). The monolith sits at **8030 bytes** — only **161 bytes of headroom**.

A minimal FOR/NEXT implementation needs:
- 4 new keywords in `ik` (FOR, TO, STEP, NEXT): ~130 bytes
- FOR stack var decl (4 parallel arrays + fp): ~50 bytes
- FOR dispatch (parse `V=expr TO expr [STEP expr]`, push): ~250 bytes
- NEXT dispatch (increment, compare with sign-aware test, loop or pop): ~180 bytes
- `fp:=0` init in 2 places: ~12 bytes

≈ **620 bytes** needed vs. 161 available. I can probably squeeze another 50–80 bytes out of existing code with aggressive compression, but not 450+.

The plan anticipated this: steps 018–019 split the monolith into p-code units to lift the constraint, gated on `sw-cor24-pcode` load-time resolution.

**Options — how would you like to proceed?**

1. **Jump ahead to 018/019** (unit build pipeline + split monolith), then come back to FOR/NEXT with room to spare. Requires `sw-cor24-pcode` unit system to be ready — last check had it blocked.
2. **Patch p24p's `INPUT_BUF_SIZE`** in `sw-cor24-pascal` (one-line change to `p24p.s:11438` + bust `build/p24p.bin` cache). Cross-repo but surgical; unblocks several steps.
3. **Ship a reduced FOR/NEXT** — e.g., no STEP keyword, STEP always 1, positive loops only. Violates the step description.
4. **Skip to a different step** that fits within budget.

My recommendation: **#2** — it's the smallest change that unblocks real work, and the unit-split (#1) is a bigger detour that itself depends on upstream pcode work.

content.text:
Both ran cleanly to exit 0:

**hello.bas:**
```
HELLO WORLD
```

**calc.bas:**
```
COR24 BASIC V1
2+3=5
7*6=42
100/7=14
A=10
B=20
A+B=30
(A+B)*2=60
ABS(-42)=10
```

No hangs here. Two notes:

1. **`ABS(-42)=10` is wrong** — ABS isn't wired into `ik` (only declared in `basic_tokens.pas` idx 23), so the tokenizer falls through, reads `A` as variable A (which was set to 10 earlier), and ignores the rest. Pre-existing bug, not from this step.
2. If it hangs on your side, likely cause is `read_line` blocking on EOF because `run-basic.sh` pipes the file via `-i "$(cat ...)"` without a trailing EOT (`\x04`) or newline. Works here because `pv24t` closes input when the `-i` arg is consumed. Want me to harden `run-basic.sh` to append `\x04` + use a timeout-safe exit?

content.text: Found it. `"$(cat file)"` **strips the trailing newline**, so `read_line` never sees `\n` after `BYE` and blocks on the next UART read. The `-n` cap eventually expires but it takes long enough to appear as a hang. Fix the runner to preserve the newline and append EOT.

#!/bin/bash
# run-basic.sh — Run a .bas file through the BASIC interpreter
# Usage: ./scripts/run-basic.sh examples/hello.bas
#
# Feeds the .bas file as UART input to the compiled interpreter.
# Uses pv24t (host-side p-code trace interpreter).
set -euo pipefail

BAS="${1:?Usage: $0 <file.bas>}"
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
PV24T="$REPO_DIR/../sw-cor24-pcode/target/release/pv24t"
P24="$REPO_DIR/build/basic.p24"

if [ ! -f "$P24" ]; then
  echo "build/basic.p24 not found — run ./scripts/build-basic.sh first" >&2
  exit 1
fi

# Feed .bas content as UART input, strip all > prompts
"$PV24T" "$P24" -i "$(cat "$BAS")" -n 10000000 2>&1 | tr -d '>' | grep -v '^$'

content.text:
Fixed. Both run and exit instantly now.

**Root cause:** `"$(cat file)"` strips trailing newlines in shell command substitution, so after `BYE` there was no line terminator. `read_line` kept waiting on UART for EOL/EOT, and only the `-n 10000000` instruction cap eventually halted it — long enough to look like a hang.

**Fix:** `scripts/run-basic.sh` now appends `\n\x04` (newline + EOT) to the input. `read_line` treats `\x04` as a line terminator, so the final line is always delivered even for files missing a trailing newline.

(Also noticed: `calc.bas` shows `ABS(-42)=10` — ABS isn't wired into `ik`, so tokenizer sees it as variable `A` (=10) plus junk. Pre-existing, unrelated. Flag for step 016 polish.)

message.content: ELI5 how the basic is run (and how the interpreter is run)

content.text:
# ELI5: How COR24 BASIC runs

Imagine a Russian nesting doll with 4 layers. Each layer doesn't know or care what's inside it — it just runs the layer below.

## The layers

**Layer 1 — The chip (emulated).** COR24 is a pretend computer. We don't have the real hardware, so `cor24-emu` (a Rust program) pretends to be one. It has registers, memory, and a serial port (UART) — just like a real chip.

**Layer 2 — The p-code VM.** Sitting on top of the chip is a tiny virtual machine called "p-code" (think: Java bytecode, but simpler). It has its own instructions like `push`, `add`, `call`. `pv24t` is a host-side shortcut that runs p-code directly without booting the chip emulator — faster for dev.

**Layer 3 — The BASIC interpreter.** This is our `basic.pas` file. It's a Pascal program that, when running, reads BASIC text (`PRINT "HI"`), figures out what it means, and does it. But Pascal doesn't run on a chip directly — it has to be compiled first.

**Layer 4 — Your BASIC program.** `hello.bas` is just text. The interpreter in Layer 3 reads it character by character and executes it.

## How we **build** the interpreter

```
basic.pas  →  [p24p]  →  basic.spc  →  [pl24r + pa24r]  →  basic.p24
 (Pascal)     compiler    (assembly)      (link+assemble)    (p-code)
```

1. **`p24p`** is a Pascal compiler — but *it* is itself a p-code program! So to compile `basic.pas`, we run `p24p.bin` on the emulator, feeding `basic.pas` text into its UART as if someone were typing it. p24p reads that text and spits out p-code assembly (`basic.spc`) on its UART output. **This is why the 8KB source limit exists** — p24p's input buffer is 8192 bytes.
2. **`pl24r`** links `basic.spc` with the Pascal runtime (helpers like readln, writeln).
3. **`pa24r`** assembles the text to a binary `basic.p24`.
4. A `sed` patch swaps p24p's default `readln` for a simpler one-char-at-a-time UART read (so our REPL sees each keystroke).

That whole chain is what `./scripts/build-basic.sh` does.

## How we **run** the interpreter

```
hello.bas text  →  [pv24t feeds it as UART input]  →  basic.p24 runs  →  output
```

1. **`pv24t`** loads `basic.p24` (our compiled interpreter).
2. The `.bas` file's text is handed to `pv24t` via `-i`, which pipes it into the virtual UART — as if you were typing it at a terminal.
3. The interpreter wakes up, shows its `>` prompt, reads characters one at a time via UART, builds a line, tokenizes it, and dispatches (`PRINT` → print, `LET` → assign, `BYE` → stop).
4. When it sees `BYE`, it sets `running:=0`, the main loop exits, the p-code program returns, and `pv24t` exits.

`./scripts/demo-hello.sh` just wraps all of that: it calls `run-basic.sh examples/hello.bas`, which launches `pv24t` with `basic.p24` and feeds it the file contents (plus the `\n\x04` we added last round so the last line terminates cleanly).

## The mind-bender

The Pascal compiler (`p24p`) is itself a p-code program — meaning **we run one p-code program (the compiler) to produce another p-code program (the BASIC interpreter), which then runs your BASIC program as text input**. Three nested interpreters deep. That's dogfooding the toolchain: the same VM runs both the compiler and the thing it compiles.

message.content:
../web-sw-cor24-basic disagrees with you. it claims: 
The CLI uses pv24t — a Rust host-side p-code interpreter, NOT the emulator running pvm.s. So there's no .s file involved in the CLI at all.
The question is: for the browser version, do we use pv24t-style direct Rust p-code interpretation (what the broken runner.rs currently does), or do we use the emulator + pvm.s approach (like web-sw-cor24-pascal does)?
so who is wrong, you or the broken web ui agent?  exactly which .s file do you use to run the p-code VM (in order to run the interpreter, in order to run BASIC demos)?

content.text:
The web-UI agent is right; I was wrong. Let me correct the record precisely.

**Exact runtime path for `./scripts/demo-hello.sh`:**

```
demo-hello.sh → run-basic.sh → pv24t build/basic.p24 -i <bas text>
```

`pv24t` is a **Rust** host-side p-code interpreter (`sw-cor24-pcode/target/release/pv24t`, source in Rust). It reads `.p24` files and executes p-code opcodes directly in native Rust. **No `.s` file is loaded, no emulator is spawned, no `pvm.s` is involved.** Zero assembly in the run path.

**Build path** (`build-basic.sh`) — also doesn't use `pvm.s`:

```
cor24-emu --load-binary p24p.bin@0 --entry 0 -u "<basic.pas text>"
```

Here the emulator runs `p24p.bin`, which is `sw-cor24-pascal/compiler/p24p.s` (the Pascal compiler written in **native COR24 assembly**) assembled to a binary. p24p emits p-code assembly to UART; `pl24r` and `pa24r` (both Rust) then link and assemble it into `basic.p24`. Still no `pvm.s` — because p24p itself is native COR24 code, not p-code.

**So: `pvm.s` is not used anywhere in the CLI build or run of BASIC demos.** The only `.s` file in the chain is `p24p.s`, and only during build (to compile `basic.pas`). At runtime, it's pure Rust (`pv24t`) interpreting `basic.p24`.

**Where I was wrong in the ELI5:** I said "Layer 1 is the chip emulated by cor24-emu" and described pv24t as "a shortcut that skips it." That framing implied the emulator/pvm.s path is the canonical one and pv24t is an optimization. For the CLI BASIC flow that's backwards — pv24t *is* the path, and `cor24-emu` only shows up during build to host the native-assembly Pascal compiler.

**For your web-UI decision:** the CLI precedent is Rust p-code interpretation (`pv24t`-style), not emulator+pvm.s. If `web-sw-cor24-pascal` is doing emulator+pvm.s, that's a different architectural choice — probably because Pascal's web demo wants to show native COR24 execution, whereas BASIC's CLI skips that layer for speed. Matching the CLI means porting `pv24t`'s Rust p-code interpreter to WASM, not running `pvm.s` on a wasm'd emulator.

message.content: what is needed to fix the calc demo abs bug?

content.text:
Two changes to `src/basic.pas`:

**1. Register the keyword** in `ik` so the tokenizer matches `ABS`:
```pascal
ks(23,'A','B','S',' ',' ',' ');
```
Slot 23 already exists in `basic_tokens.pas` and `NK=24` already covers it — the tokenizer is iterating the slot but `kt[23*KW..]` is uninitialized garbage, so `kl(23)` doesn't return a usable length and matches fail. Initializing the slot fixes that.

**2. Handle it in `p_expr` level 0** as a unary function call:
```pascal
else if tb[ep]=FK+23 then begin ep:=ep+1;
  if tb[ep]=176 then begin ep:=ep+1;p_expr(4);v:=ev;
    if v<0 then v:=0-v;
    if(err=0)and(tb[ep]=177)then ep:=ep+1 else if err=0 then err:=1
  end else err:=1 end
```
This belongs alongside the existing `tb[ep]=176` parenthesis case in level 0 (lines ~111). It consumes `ABS`, requires `(`, recursively parses the inner expression, takes the absolute value, and requires `)`.

**Cost:** ~200 bytes added. Current monolith is 8030, headroom is 161 — so this is **~40 bytes over budget** and lands in the same wall as FOR/NEXT. Smaller than FOR/NEXT (which needed ~620), but still requires either:
- Squeezing ~40 bytes out of existing code, or
- The same upstream fix (enlarge p24p `INPUT_BUF_SIZE`, or move to units).

A 40-byte squeeze is plausible — e.g., the `print_int` proc and the long single-char-token if/else chain in `tokenize` both have small wins available. Want me to try?

message.content: what limit is being reached? what layer/repo enforces this limit? Can I increase it, and if so, where?

content.text:
**The limit:** 8192 bytes of Pascal source code.

**What enforces it:** the `p24p` Pascal compiler's input buffer. p24p reads the entire `.pas` source from its UART into a fixed-size array, then parses it. If the source is longer than the buffer, the tail gets dropped.

**Where it lives — two places, both in `sw-cor24-pascal`:**

1. **C source of truth** — `sw-cor24-pascal/compiler/src/main.c:9`
   ```c
   #define INPUT_BUF_SIZE 8192
   char input_buf[INPUT_BUF_SIZE];
   ```
   This is the canonical definition. p24p was originally written in C and cross-compiled to COR24 assembly.

2. **Assembled artifact** — `sw-cor24-pascal/compiler/p24p.s:11438`
   ```
   la r0,8192
   ```
   This is the compiled-down version that actually runs. It's the bound-check in the input-read loop. There may be other constants nearby (buffer base address, end-of-buffer compare) that move together with this number.

**What runs it:** `build-basic.sh` assembles `p24p.s` to `build/p24p.bin` (cached) and runs it on `cor24-emu`, feeding `basic.pas` into the emulator's UART. So the `p24p.s:11438` value is what actually limits a BASIC build.

**To increase it — three options, in order of effort:**

1. **Hot-patch `p24p.s`** (smallest change). Edit line 11438 from `8192` to e.g. `16384` or `32768`, then `rm sw-cor24-pascal/compiler/p24p.bin` (or `sw-cor24-basic/build/p24p.bin`) so `build-basic.sh` re-assembles it. **Caveat:** you also need to confirm the actual array `input_buf` in `p24p.s` has room — increasing the bound check without enlarging the underlying storage would corrupt adjacent memory. Look near the `.bss`/`.data` directives in `p24p.s` for `input_buf` (or whatever it's named after C-to-asm mangling) and check its reservation.

2. **Edit C source and rebuild p24p** (clean change). Bump `INPUT_BUF_SIZE` in `compiler/src/main.c:9`, then re-cross-compile to `p24p.s`. This requires the C→COR24 toolchain (`sw-cor24-x-tinyc` per the CLAUDE.md cross-repo list) and knowledge of how `p24p.s` was originally produced. Worth checking `sw-cor24-pascal/compiler/justfile` or `scripts/` for an existing rebuild target.

3. **Skip the limit entirely via units** (the planned fix). Steps 018–019 split `basic.pas` into multiple unit files compiled separately, so no individual file ever approaches 8KB. Blocked on `sw-cor24-pcode` load-time-resolution work.

**Recommendation if you want fast unblock:** option 1 — patch `p24p.s:11438`, verify the buffer reservation has space (or grow it too), bust the cached `p24p.bin`, rebuild BASIC. It's a five-minute change in a sibling repo and unblocks ABS, FOR/NEXT, and the rest of the v1 feature set without waiting on the unit system.

Want me to investigate `p24p.s` to find the `input_buf` reservation and confirm whether option 1 is a one-line or two-line patch?

content.content:
title:	p24p: Add unit compilation mode (--unit flag)
state:	CLOSED
author:	softwarewrighter (Software Wrighter LLC)
labels:	
comments:	0
assignees:	
projects:	
milestone:	
number:	1
--
## Summary

p24p needs a mode to compile Pascal source as a p-code unit rather than a standalone program, enabling multi-unit builds via p24-load.

A --unit flag that changes .spc output: .module → .unit, adds .export for all user procedures, uses .extern/xcall for cross-unit calls.

## Why This Is Needed

The BASIC interpreter has ~8KB+ of Pascal source across 5+ modules. The p-code unit system is implemented in sw-cor24-pcode (pa24r v2 format, p24-load, pvm.s .p24m support). The missing piece is the compiler emitting unit directives.

## Workaround

Post-process .spc output: sed .module → .unit, add .export lines. Fragile but functional for initial integration.

Migrated from softwarewrighter/p24c#9.

content.text:
Good news — **there's nothing left to fix in `sw-cor24-pcode`**. Both blocker issues are already CLOSED:

- **`sw-cor24-pcode`** issues #1 and #2 closed. Saga steps 001–012 (memcpy/memset, xcall, p24 v2 format, p24-loader, multi-unit VM init, linker `--unit` mode, xloadg/xstoreg, integration tests, p24-dump) **all complete**. Step 013 in progress is unrelated (vm_flags). The whole load-time-resolution stack is shipped.
- **`sw-cor24-pascal`** issue #1 (`p24p: Add unit compilation mode --unit flag`) **CLOSED**. Verified in `compiler/src/parser.c:1709` — `--unit` is implemented and emits `.unit`/`.endunit`/`.import p24p_rt`/`.export main`.

So the upstream side of "option 3" is already done. The remaining work is **entirely in `sw-cor24-basic`** — exactly the two steps already on your plan:

- **Step 018** — refactor `build-basic.sh` to compile each `.pas` source with `p24p --unit`, then link via `p24-load` into a single `.p24m` image. (Note: the build currently feeds `basic.pas` into p24p via UART. With `--unit` mode you'll need a way to pass the flag to the compiler — either via a command-line arg the emulator surfaces, or by patching `unit_mode` in `p24p.s`.)
- **Step 019** — split `src/basic.pas` into separate unit source files. The skeleton already exists in the repo (`basic_tokens.pas`, `basic_lex.pas`, `basic_store.pas`, `basic_expr.pas`, `basic_stmt.pas`) — they were created in steps 003–007 but aren't in the current build. They need to be revived, made to compile cleanly under `--unit`, and wired through `build-basic.sh`.

**Recommendation:** jump the agentrail saga ahead to step 018 next session. The 8KB ceiling disappears, ABS/FOR/NEXT all become trivially fittable, and you stop fighting the byte budget.

If you genuinely want a `sw-cor24-pcode` issue to work on, check step 013 (`vm-boot-flags`) which is in progress there — but that's unrelated to BASIC's blocker.

content.text:
**Current step:** 013 FOR/NEXT  
**Next:** 014 PRINT formatting  
**Steps before 018:** 5 (013, 014, 015, 016, 017)

**Blocked status:**

| # | Step | Fits in 161-byte budget? |
|---|---|---|
| 013 | FOR/NEXT | ❌ ~620 bytes needed |
| 014 | PRINT formatting (column tracking, tab stops) | ⚠️ ~150 bytes — borderline |
| 015 | INPUT enhancements | ❌ INPUT itself isn't even implemented yet (deferred to step 020); can't enhance what doesn't exist |
| 016 | Error polish, banner, REM in LIST | ⚠️ ~100 bytes — borderline |
| 017 | Hardware demos (.bas files, test harness) | ✅ no source-size impact |

So **I am blocked** on 013 and 015, and 014/016 are coin-flips that risk eating headroom needed for the next step. Proceeding "normally" through 017 would mean either breaking the 8KB ceiling (interpreter silently truncates) or doing tiny incremental compressions of existing code that'd be wasted work once 018/019 lift the ceiling.

**Two paths forward:**

1. **Reorder the saga**: do 018 → 019 first (lift the ceiling), then 013–017 in order with no budget worries. Cleanest. Requires editing the agentrail plan to reorder.
2. **Push through anyway**: I attempt 013 with aggressive squeezing, possibly skip 015, try 014/016/017 one at a time. Likely produces churn and brittle compressions that get thrown away in 019.

I recommend **#1**. Want me to reorder the saga and start step 018 next?

message.content: so open an issue to bump the 8k limit to 16k

input.command:
gh issue create --repo sw-embed/sw-cor24-pascal --title "p24p: bump INPUT_BUF_SIZE from 8192 to 16384" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

Increase the Pascal compiler's source input buffer from 8KB to 16KB to unblock larger single-file Pascal sources.

## Why

`sw-cor24-basic` is hitting the 8192-byte ceiling on its monolithic `src/basic.pas` (currently 8030 bytes). Several upcoming v1 features cannot be added without going over:

- Step 013 (FOR/NEXT): needs ~620 bytes
- Step 014 (PRINT formatting): ~150 bytes
- Step 016 (error polish): ~100 bytes
- Plus a fix for the ABS bug in `examples/calc.bas` (~200 bytes)

The long-term fix is unit splitting (sw-cor24-basic steps 018–019, now unblocked since `p24p --unit` and the pcode loader are shipped). Bumping the buffer to 16KB is a stopgap that lets the basic interpreter complete v1 features in monolithic form while the unit refactor is scheduled.

## Where

Two places need to move together:

1. **C source of truth** — `compiler/src/main.c:9`
   ```c
   #define INPUT_BUF_SIZE 8192
   ```
2. **Assembled artifact** — `compiler/p24p.s:11438`
   ```
   la r0,8192
   ```
   The underlying `input_buf` storage reservation in `p24p.s` also needs to be enlarged to match — bumping only the bound check would corrupt adjacent memory.

## Acceptance

- `INPUT_BUF_SIZE` is 16384 in `main.c`.
- `p24p.s` is regenerated (or hot-patched) so both the bound check and `input_buf` reservation reflect 16KB.
- Existing p24p tests pass.
- A 10KB Pascal source compiles successfully (regression test).

## Alternatives Considered

- **Wait for unit split** (sw-cor24-basic #018/#019): correct long-term fix, but blocks immediate BASIC v1 progress.
- **Patch only `p24p.s`**: faster but leaves C source out of sync.
EOF
)" 2>&1

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(repl): add FOR...TO...STEP and NEXT statements

FOR pushes a frame onto a 16-slot stack with parallel arrays for
loop variable index, limit, step, and restart cursor (fv/fl/fs/fr).
STEP defaults to 1 when omitted; negative steps are supported via
sign-aware bound check in NEXT.

NEXT pops the top frame, increments the loop variable by step, and
either restores tl to the restart cursor (if still in range) or
discards the frame. RUN resets fp.

New error codes: 8 (FOR stack overflow), 9 (NEXT without FOR).

Headroom from sw-cor24-pascal#2 (INPUT_BUF_SIZE 8192->16384) made
this fit cleanly without further compression; source is now 8955
bytes.

Tested: FOR 1 TO 5, FOR 10 TO 1 STEP -1, FOR 2 TO 10 STEP 2,
nested FOR I/J producing 11 12 21 22 31 32, bare NEXT err 9,
hello/calc demos still green.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push origin main 2>&1 | tail -3

input.command:
gh issue create --repo sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic --title "ABS function silently returns wrong value (parsed as variable A)" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

`ABS(expr)` doesn't work — the tokenizer falls through to variable parsing and returns the value of variable `A` instead of the absolute value of the argument.

## Reproduction

\`\`\`
LET A=10
PRINT "ABS(-42)=";ABS(-42)
\`\`\`

Output:
\`\`\`
ABS(-42)=10
\`\`\`

Expected: \`ABS(-42)=42\`

Visible in \`./scripts/demo-calc.sh\`.

## Root cause

\`ABS\` is declared in \`src/basic_tokens.pas\` at slot 23 but is **not** initialized in the \`ik\` (init keywords) procedure of \`src/basic.pas\`. The tokenizer iterates slots 0..NK-1 calling \`kl(k)\` to get the keyword length, but for slot 23 the underlying \`kt\` storage is uninitialized garbage, so no match ever succeeds.

The tokenizer then falls through to the variable case, consuming \`A\` as variable A. The remaining \`BS(-42)\` tokenizes as junk that the dispatcher silently ignores after the expression parser stops.

## Fix

Two changes to \`src/basic.pas\`:

1. **Register the keyword** in \`ik\`:
   \`\`\`pascal
   ks(23,'A','B','S',' ',' ',' ');
   \`\`\`

2. **Handle it in \`p_expr\` level 0** as a unary function call alongside the existing \`(\` case:
   \`\`\`pascal
   else if tb[ep]=FK+23 then begin ep:=ep+1;
     if tb[ep]=176 then begin ep:=ep+1;p_expr(4);v:=ev;
       if v<0 then v:=0-v;
       if(err=0)and(tb[ep]=177)then ep:=ep+1 else if err=0 then err:=1
     end else err:=1 end
   \`\`\`

## Acceptance

- \`ABS(-42)\` returns 42
- \`ABS(42)\` returns 42
- \`ABS(0)\` returns 0
- \`ABS(A-B)\` evaluates inner expression first
- \`./scripts/demo-calc.sh\` shows \`ABS(-42)=42\`

## Notes

Pre-existing bug, not introduced by FOR/NEXT or PRINT formatting work. Now fittable within source-size budget thanks to the p24p INPUT_BUF_SIZE bump (sw-cor24-pascal#2).
EOF
)" 2>&1

feat(repl): implement INPUT statement with prompt and ?REDO

INPUT was originally deferred to step 020 (after the planned unit
split) due to the 8KB source ceiling. With p24p's input buffer
bumped to 16KB (sw-cor24-pascal#2) it fits cleanly here, so step
015 now ships INPUT itself plus all the planned enhancements.

Syntax:
  INPUT V          { default "? " prompt }
  INPUT "msg"; V   { custom prompt, no newline after }
  INPUT "msg", V   { comma form also accepted }

A new pi (parse-integer) helper reads lb/ll, accepts optional +/-
sign, and exposes pok=1 on success. On parse failure INPUT prints
"?REDO " and re-reads from the UART, looping until a valid integer
arrives. Negative numbers parse correctly.

Tested: prompted read with positive value, default-prompt read
with negative value (-7 -> -14 after *2), bad-then-good input
("foo" then "5") shows ?REDO and continues. hello/calc demos
still green. Source 10084 bytes.

Refs: sw-cor24-basic step 015

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push origin main 2>&1 | tail -3

toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/bin/bash
# run-basic.sh — Run a .bas file through the BASIC interpreter
# Usage: ./scripts/run-basic.sh examples/hello.bas
#
# Feeds the .bas file as UART input to the compiled interpreter.
# Uses pv24t (host-side p-code trace interpreter).
set -euo pipefail

BAS="${1:?Usage: $0 <file.bas>}"
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
PV24T="$REPO_DIR/../sw-cor24-pcode/target/release/pv24t"
P24="$REPO_DIR/build/basic.p24"

if [ ! -f "$P24" ]; then
  echo "build/basic.p24 not found — run ./scripts/build-basic.sh first" >&2
  exit 1
fi

# Feed .bas content as UART input, strip all > prompts.
# Append EOT (\x04) so the final line terminates cleanly even if the
# file lacks a trailing newline after BYE — otherwise read_line blocks
# waiting for more UART input and the run appears to hang.
INPUT="$(cat "$BAS")"$'\n\x04'
"$PV24T" "$P24" -i "$INPUT" -n 10000000 2>&1 | tr -d '>' | grep -v '^$'

lines: # file lacks a trailing newline after BYE — otherwise read_line blocks
# waiting for more UART input and the run appears to hang.

feat(repl): polish - banner, LIST, REM, line-aware errors

Step 016 polish pass:

* Startup banner: prints "COR24 BASIC V1" + "READY" once at boot.
* LIST command (re-)implemented as do_list. Walks the program
  store and detokenizes each line. Uses a last-class (lc) tracker
  so keywords/identifiers/integers separate cleanly without
  cluttering operator-adjacent tokens (LET A=5, A>0 THEN, etc.).
* REM bodies reproduce verbatim in LIST without quotes.
* REM is now a no-op at runtime (dispatch case for FK+14) so
  RUN no longer reports ?ERR 2 IN <line> on REM lines.
* Errors include the executing line number when raised inside
  RUN: "?ERR 5 IN 10". Tracked via new global el, set in the
  exec branch of the main loop and cleared in REPL mode.
* Fix run-basic.sh prompt-stripping: tr -d '>' was eating the
  '>' operator anywhere in output. Replaced with sed -E
  's/^>+//' which only strips leading prompt chars.
* Renamed local kw -> kn in do_list to avoid shadowing the
  case-insensitive KW constant.

Source 11987 bytes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push origin main 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text: All three demos run. Now `docs/demos.md`:

input.content:
# COR24 BASIC — Demo Programs

A small gallery of programs that exercise the v1 interpreter. Each
demo has a matching `scripts/demo-<name>.sh` wrapper that builds (if
needed) and runs the corresponding `examples/<name>.bas` through
`pv24t`, the host-side p-code interpreter.

## Running

```sh
./scripts/build-basic.sh        # one-time, or after editing src/
./scripts/demo-hello.sh
./scripts/demo-calc.sh
./scripts/demo-fizzbuzz.sh
./scripts/demo-fibonacci.sh
./scripts/demo-factorial.sh
```

Each script feeds the `.bas` file to the interpreter as if it were
typed at the REPL. The files include trailing `RUN` and `BYE` lines
so they run end-to-end and exit cleanly.

## Demo Index

| Script | File | What it shows |
|---|---|---|
| `demo-hello.sh` | `examples/hello.bas` | Immediate-mode `PRINT` and `BYE`. The smallest possible program. |
| `demo-calc.sh` | `examples/calc.bas` | Arithmetic, `LET`, mixed string/expression `PRINT`. *(Note: shows the open ABS bug — see issue #1.)* |
| `demo-fizzbuzz.sh` | `examples/fizzbuzz.bas` | `FOR`/`NEXT`, `IF`...`THEN GOTO`, divisibility via `(I/N)*N=I`, multi-target dispatch. |
| `demo-fibonacci.sh` | `examples/fibonacci.bas` | Iterative two-variable swap inside `FOR`/`NEXT`. First 10 Fibonacci numbers. |
| `demo-factorial.sh` | `examples/factorial.bas` | Iterative product accumulator inside `FOR`/`NEXT`. Mixed-type `PRINT` of `N;"!=";F`. |

## Demo Notes

### hello.bas

```basic
PRINT "HELLO WORLD"
BYE
```

Immediate mode — no line numbers. The simplest possible smoke test.

### calc.bas

Demonstrates arithmetic precedence (`(A+B)*2`), integer division
(`100/7=14`), and the mixed-type `PRINT` form (`PRINT "X=";X`).

> **Open bug:** the last line shows `ABS(-42)=10` instead of `42`.
> `ABS` is declared in `src/basic_tokens.pas` but not initialized in
> the `ik` keyword table, so the tokenizer falls through and consumes
> `A` as variable A. Tracked in
> [sw-cor24-basic#1](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic/issues/1)
> and queued as saga step `025-fix-abs-bug`.

### fizzbuzz.bas

There is no `MOD` operator in v1. Divisibility is tested with
`(I/N)*N = I` since integer division truncates. The dispatch chain
checks 15 first, then 3, then 5; each branch jumps to a print line
and back to `NEXT` via `GOTO`.

### fibonacci.bas

Classic three-variable rotation:

```basic
50 PRINT A
60 LET C=A+B
70 LET A=B
80 LET B=C
```

Outputs the first ten terms (0..34). Watch out for overflow: integers
are 24-bit signed, so the sequence stays valid up through the low
millions.

### factorial.bas

`N!` via straight `FOR I=1 TO N` accumulation. With `N=7` you get
`5040`. The 24-bit integer ceiling caps you at `12!` (479,001,600);
`13!` overflows.

## Recursion?

**Short answer:** no, not usefully.

COR24 BASIC v1 has `GOSUB`/`RETURN` with a 64-deep return stack, so
you *can* call a subroutine from within itself — the return addresses
nest correctly. But there are no parameters and no per-call locals:
all 26 variables (`A`..`Z`) are global. Each "recursive" call
overwrites the same storage as its caller, so by the time `RETURN`
fires, the supposed "local state" is gone.

There is also no tail-call optimization. `GOSUB` always pushes,
`RETURN` always pops; a `GOSUB` immediately followed by `RETURN`
still consumes a stack slot for the duration of the call. Every level
counts against the 64-slot ceiling.

The practical implication: write loops, not recursion. Factorial,
Fibonacci, GCD, and friends are all comfortable as iterative
`FOR`/`NEXT` or `GOTO` loops, which is exactly the style this
flavor of BASIC was designed for.

## Adding a new demo

1. Drop `examples/<name>.bas` ending with `RUN` and `BYE`.
2. Copy an existing wrapper:
   ```sh
   cp scripts/demo-hello.sh scripts/demo-<name>.sh
   sed -i '' "s/hello/<name>/g" scripts/demo-<name>.sh
   ```
3. Add a row to the table above.
4. `./scripts/demo-<name>.sh` to verify.

Demonstrates arithmetic precedence (`(A+B)*2`), integer division
(`100/7=14`), and the mixed-type `PRINT` form (`PRINT "X=";X`).

> **Open bug:** the last line shows `ABS(-42)=10` instead of `42`.
> `ABS` is declared in `src/basic_tokens.pas` but not initialized in
> the `ik` keyword table, so the tokenizer falls through and consumes
> `A` as variable A. Tracked in
> [sw-cor24-basic#1](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic/issues/1)
> and queued as saga step `025-fix-abs-bug`.

### fizzbuzz.bas

There is no `MOD` operator in v1. Divisibility is tested with
`(I/N)*N = I` since integer division truncates. The dispatch chain
checks 15 first, then 3, then 5; each branch jumps to a print line
and back to `NEXT` via `GOTO`.

### fibonacci.bas

Classic three-variable rotation:

```basic
50 PRINT A
60 LET C=A+B
70 LET A=B
80 LET B=C
```

Outputs the first ten terms (0..34). Watch out for overflow: integers
are 24-bit signed, so the sequence stays valid up through the low
millions.

### factorial.bas

`N!` via straight `FOR I=1 TO N` accumulation. With `N=7` you get
`5040`. The 24-bit integer ceiling caps you at `12!` (479,001,600);
`13!` overflows.

## Recursion?

**Short answer:** no, not usefully.

COR24 BASIC v1 has `GOSUB`/`RETURN` with a 64-deep return stack, so
you *can* call a subroutine from within itself — the return addresses
nest correctly. But there are no parameters and no per-call locals:
all 26 variables (`A`..`Z`) are global. Each "recursive" call
overwrites the same storage as its caller, so by the time `RETURN`
fires, the supposed "local state" is gone.

There is also no tail-call optimization. `GOSUB` always pushes,
`RETURN` always pops; a `GOSUB` immediately followed by `RETURN`
still consumes a stack slot for the duration of the call. Every level
counts against the 64-slot ceiling.

The practical implication: write loops, not recursion. Factorial,
Fibonacci, GCD, and friends are all comfortable as iterative
`FOR`/`NEXT` or `GOTO` loops, which is exactly the style this
flavor of BASIC was designed for.

## Adding a new demo

1. Drop `examples/<name>.bas` ending with `RUN` and `BYE`.
2. Copy an existing wrapper:
   ```sh
   cp scripts/demo-hello.sh scripts/demo-<name>.sh
   sed -i '' "s/hello/<name>/g" scripts/demo-<name>.sh
   ```
3. Add a row to the table above.
4. `./scripts/demo-<name>.sh` to verify.

docs(demos): add fizzbuzz, fibonacci, factorial demos and demos.md

Three new examples plus their CLI wrappers, and a docs/demos.md
gallery describing what each one exercises and how to run them.

* examples/fizzbuzz.bas - FOR/NEXT + IF...THEN GOTO + divisibility
  via (I/N)*N=I (no MOD operator in v1)
* examples/fibonacci.bas - iterative three-variable rotation,
  first 10 terms
* examples/factorial.bas - iterative product accumulator, 7!=5040
* scripts/demo-{fizzbuzz,fibonacci,factorial}.sh - one-line wrappers
  matching the existing demo-hello/demo-calc convention

docs/demos.md also explains why recursion isn't usefully supported
in v1: GOSUB/RETURN nests correctly via the 64-slot stack but the
26 variables A..Z are all global with no per-call frame, so there
is no place for local state. No TCO either.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push origin main 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
Done. Three new demos shipped: `fizzbuzz`, `fibonacci`, `factorial`, each with `examples/*.bas` + `scripts/demo-*.sh` wrappers. Pushed `0e2eff0`.

**Recursion verdict** (also in `docs/demos.md`): no, not usefully. `GOSUB`/`RETURN` nests correctly via the 64-slot return stack, but variables `A`..`Z` are all global — no parameters, no per-frame locals — so a "recursive" call clobbers its caller's state. No TCO either; every `GOSUB` consumes a stack slot until its matching `RETURN`. Iterative `FOR`/`NEXT` is the idiomatic style. (Pure stack of return addresses doesn't help when there's nowhere to put per-call data.)

.export _p24p_led_on
.export _p24p_led_off
.export _p24p_read_switch
.export _p24p_halt
.export _p24p_peek
.export _p24p_poke
.export _p24p_memcpy
.export _p24p_memset

; pr24p — Pascal Runtime Library
; Phase 0: Hand-written .spc stubs for p-code VM syscall wrappers
--
; _p24p_led_on ( -- )
; Turn LED on. sys 3 convention: 1 = on, 0 = off.
; (VM handles active-low inversion internally via XOR.)
.proc _p24p_led_on 0
    push 1               ; 1 = ON
    sys 3                ; LED
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_led_off ( -- )
; Turn LED off. sys 3 convention: 1 = on, 0 = off.
.proc _p24p_led_off 0
    push 0               ; 0 = OFF
    sys 3                ; LED
    ret 0
.end

--
; _p24p_peek ( addr -- byte )
; Read single byte at address via loadb.
.proc _p24p_peek 0
    loada 0              ; load addr
    loadb                ; read byte at addr
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_poke ( addr val -- )
; Write single byte to address via storeb.
; Calling convention: push addr, push val, call. loada 0=val, loada 1=addr.
.proc _p24p_poke 0
    loada 0              ; load val
    loada 1              ; load addr
    storeb               ; store byte val at addr
    ret 2
.end
[result-id: r55]

; pr24p — Pascal Runtime Library
; Phase 0: Hand-written .spc stubs for p-code VM syscall wrappers
--
; _p24p_led_on ( -- )
; Turn LED on. sys 3 convention: 1 = on, 0 = off.
; (VM handles active-low inversion internally via XOR.)
.proc _p24p_led_on 0
    push 1               ; 1 = ON
    sys 3                ; LED
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_led_off ( -- )
; Turn LED off. sys 3 convention: 1 = on, 0 = off.
.proc _p24p_led_off 0
    push 0               ; 0 = OFF
    sys 3                ; LED
    ret 0
.end

--
; _p24p_peek ( addr -- byte )
; Read single byte at address via loadb.
.proc _p24p_peek 0
    loada 0              ; load addr
    loadb                ; read byte at addr
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_poke ( addr val -- )
; Write single byte to address via storeb.
; Calling convention: push addr, push val, call. loada 0=val, loada 1=addr.
.proc _p24p_poke 0
    loada 0              ; load val
    loada 1              ; load addr
    storeb               ; store byte val at addr
    ret 2
.end

content.content:
# sw-cor24-basic

COR24 BASIC v1 — a 1970s time-sharing BASIC interpreter for the COR24
p-code VM.

## Overview

COR24 BASIC is a line-numbered, integer-only BASIC interpreter inspired
by UNIVAC 1100-series terminal BASIC with teletype and paper-tape
workflows. It runs on the COR24 p-code virtual machine and emphasizes
simplicity, hardware access (PEEK/POKE), and interactive program
development over compatibility with later BASIC standards.

## Architecture

```
BASIC interpreter (Pascal) → p24p → .spc → pa24r → .p24 → pvm.s → COR24
```

The interpreter is a Pascal program compiled to p-code. It runs on the
p-code VM as a language layer:

- **Layer 0**: COR24 hardware / emulator
- **Layer 1**: P-code VM (language-neutral abstract machine)
- **Layer 2**: BASIC runtime (I/O, line storage, stacks, PEEK/POKE)
- **Layer 3**: BASIC interpreter (tokenizer, parser, dispatch, execution)

## Dialect: COR24 BASIC v1

- Line-numbered stored programs, tokenized on entry
- Signed 24-bit integer arithmetic (no floating point)
- Interactive: immediate mode + stored program mode (RUN)
- Terminal/teletype oriented
- Statements: LET, PRINT, INPUT, IF...THEN, GOTO, GOSUB, RETURN,
  FOR...TO...STEP, NEXT, STOP, END, REM
- Commands: LIST, RUN, NEW, BYE
- Functions: PEEK, POKE, ABS
- Variables: A-Z (26 scalar integers)

## Positioning

BASIC complements Pascal in the COR24 ecosystem:

| | Pascal | BASIC |
|---|--------|-------|
| Execution | Compiled to p-code | Interpreted on p-code VM |
| Style | Structured, typed | Dynamic, interactive |
| Use case | Application development | Hardware bring-up, scripting, monitor |

## Status

**In progress** — tokenizer implemented and tested.

Pipeline: `.pas → p24p → .spc → pl24r → pa24r → .p24 → pv24t`

- `src/basic_tokens.pas` — token definitions, keyword table, detokenizer
- `src/basic_lex.pas` — tokenizer (keywords, integers, strings, variables,
  operators, delimiters, REM, line numbers)

Known toolchain limitations:
[result-id: r57]

# COR24 BASIC — Demo Programs

A small gallery of programs that exercise the v1 interpreter. Each
demo has a matching `scripts/demo-<name>.sh` wrapper that builds (if
needed) and runs the corresponding `examples/<name>.bas` through
`pv24t`, the host-side p-code interpreter.

## Running

```sh
./scripts/build-basic.sh        # one-time, or after editing src/
./scripts/demo-hello.sh
./scripts/demo-calc.sh
./scripts/demo-fizzbuzz.sh
./scripts/demo-fibonacci.sh
./scripts/demo-factorial.sh
```

Each script feeds the `.bas` file to the interpreter as if it were
typed at the REPL. The files include trailing `RUN` and `BYE` lines
so they run end-to-end and exit cleanly.

## Demo Index

| Script | File | What it shows |
|---|---|---|
| `demo-hello.sh` | `examples/hello.bas` | Immediate-mode `PRINT` and `BYE`. The smallest possible program. |
| `demo-calc.sh` | `examples/calc.bas` | Arithmetic, `LET`, mixed string/expression `PRINT`. *(Note: shows the open ABS bug — see issue #1.)* |
| `demo-fizzbuzz.sh` | `examples/fizzbuzz.bas` | `FOR`/`NEXT`, `IF`...`THEN GOTO`, divisibility via `(I/N)*N=I`, multi-target dispatch. |
| `demo-fibonacci.sh` | `examples/fibonacci.bas` | Iterative two-variable swap inside `FOR`/`NEXT`. First 10 Fibonacci numbers. |
| `demo-factorial.sh` | `examples/factorial.bas` | Iterative product accumulator inside `FOR`/`NEXT`. Mixed-type `PRINT` of `N;"!=";F`. |

## Demo Notes

### hello.bas

```basic
PRINT "HELLO WORLD"
BYE
```

Immediate mode — no line numbers. The simplest possible smoke test.

### calc.bas

Demonstrates arithmetic precedence (`(A+B)*2`), integer division
(`100/7=14`), and the mixed-type `PRINT` form (`PRINT "X=";X`).

> **Open bug:** the last line shows `ABS(-42)=10` instead of `42`.
> `ABS` is declared in `src/basic_tokens.pas` but not initialized in
> the `ik` keyword table, so the tokenizer falls through and consumes
> `A` as variable A. Tracked in
> [sw-cor24-basic#1](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic/issues/1)
> and queued as saga step `025-fix-abs-bug`.

### fizzbuzz.bas

There is no `MOD` operator in v1. Divisibility is tested with
`(I/N)*N = I` since integer division truncates. The dispatch chain
checks 15 first, then 3, then 5; each branch jumps to a print line
and back to `NEXT` via `GOTO`.

### fibonacci.bas

Classic three-variable rotation:

```basic
50 PRINT A
60 LET C=A+B
70 LET A=B
80 LET B=C
```

Outputs the first ten terms (0..34). Watch out for overflow: integers
are 24-bit signed, so the sequence stays valid up through the low
millions.

### factorial.bas

`N!` via straight `FOR I=1 TO N` accumulation. With `N=7` you get
`5040`. The 24-bit integer ceiling caps you at `12!` (479,001,600);
`13!` overflows.

## Recursion?

**Short answer:** no, not usefully.

COR24 BASIC v1 has `GOSUB`/`RETURN` with a 64-deep return stack, so
you *can* call a subroutine from within itself — the return addresses
nest correctly. But there are no parameters and no per-call locals:
all 26 variables (`A`..`Z`) are global. Each "recursive" call
overwrites the same storage as its caller, so by the time `RETURN`
fires, the supposed "local state" is gone.

There is also no tail-call optimization. `GOSUB` always pushes,
`RETURN` always pops; a `GOSUB` immediately followed by `RETURN`
still consumes a stack slot for the duration of the call. Every level
counts against the 64-slot ceiling.

The practical implication: write loops, not recursion. Factorial,
Fibonacci, GCD, and friends are all comfortable as iterative
`FOR`/`NEXT` or `GOTO` loops, which is exactly the style this
flavor of BASIC was designed for.

## Adding a new demo

1. Drop `examples/<name>.bas` ending with `RUN` and `BYE`.
2. Copy an existing wrapper:
   ```sh
   cp scripts/demo-hello.sh scripts/demo-<name>.sh
   sed -i '' "s/hello/<name>/g" scripts/demo-<name>.sh
   ```
3. Add a row to the table above.
4. `./scripts/demo-<name>.sh` to verify.

lines: | `demo-fibonacci.sh` | `examples/fibonacci.bas` | Iterative two-variable swap inside `FOR`/`NEXT`. First 10 Fibonacci numbers. |
| `demo-factorial.sh` | `examples/factorial.bas` | Iterative product accumulator inside `FOR`/`NEXT`. Mixed-type `PRINT` of `N;"!=";F`. |
+| `demo-memdump.sh` | `examples/memdump.bas` | `POKE` then `PEEK` round-trip on a low scratch address. Demonstrates the byte-MMIO syntax. |
+| *(no wrapper)* | `examples/blink.bas` | LED blink loop targeting the COR24 LED MMIO at `0xFF0000`. **Hardware/cor24-emu only** — `pv24t` traps because the address is outside its VM memory. |

input.old_string:
### factorial.bas

`N!` via straight `FOR I=1 TO N` accumulation. With `N=7` you get
`5040`. The 24-bit integer ceiling caps you at `12!` (479,001,600);
`13!` overflows.
input.new_string:
### factorial.bas

`N!` via straight `FOR I=1 TO N` accumulation. With `N=7` you get
`5040`. The 24-bit integer ceiling caps you at `12!` (479,001,600);
`13!` overflows.

### count.bas

The smallest stored-program example: `FOR I=1 TO 10 / PRINT I / NEXT`.
Useful as a baseline when poking at the build pipeline.

### memdump.bas

Pokes the bytes `72`, `73`, `33` ('H', 'I', '!') into addresses
`100..102`, then loops with `PEEK(I)` to print each byte:

```
100=72
101=73
102=33
```

The address range is just regular `pv24t` VM memory; no MMIO. The
demo exists to show that the language form `POKE addr,val` and
`PEEK(addr)` round-trips correctly. For real MMIO targets, see
`blink.bas`.

### blink.bas — hardware only

`POKE 16711680,0` then `POKE 16711680,1` toggles bit 0 at COR24's
`IO_LEDSWDAT` register (`0xFF0000`), where LED D2 lives. On real
hardware (or `cor24-emu`) this physically blinks the LED.

**Under `pv24t` it traps.** The host-side p-code interpreter only
allocates linear VM memory and `0xFF0000` is far outside the
allocated region. There is intentionally **no `demo-blink.sh`
wrapper** — running it would just trap. To exercise it, target the
emulator/hardware p-code VM (`pvm.s`) instead of `pv24t`. That path
isn't wired into `build-basic.sh` yet; it'll come with the unit
build pipeline (steps 018–019) which produces a `.p24m` image
loadable by `cor24-emu`.

Same caveat applies to `LED`/switch-style demos in general: under
`pv24t` the LED syscall (`sys 3`) is a no-op and `READ_SWITCH`
(`sys 6`) always returns 0.

## Recursion?

**Short answer:** no, not usefully.

COR24 BASIC v1 has `GOSUB`/`RETURN` with a 64-deep return stack, so
you *can* call a subroutine from within itself — the return addresses
nest correctly. But there are no parameters and no per-call locals:
all 26 variables (`A`..`Z`) are global. Each "recursive" call
overwrites the same storage as its caller, so by the time `RETURN`
fires, the supposed "local state" is gone.

There is also no tail-call optimization. `GOSUB` always pushes,
`RETURN` always pops; a `GOSUB` immediately followed by `RETURN`
still consumes a stack slot for the duration of the call. Every level
counts against the 64-slot ceiling.

The practical implication: write loops, not recursion. Factorial,
Fibonacci, GCD, and friends are all comfortable as iterative
`FOR`/`NEXT` or `GOTO` loops, which is exactly the style this
flavor of BASIC was designed for.

## Adding a new demo

1. Drop `examples/<name>.bas` ending with `RUN` and `BYE`.
2. Copy an existing wrapper:
   ```sh
   cp scripts/demo-hello.sh scripts/demo-<name>.sh
   sed -i '' "s/hello/<name>/g" scripts/demo-<name>.sh
   ```
3. Add a row to the table above.
4. `./scripts/demo-<name>.sh` to verify.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# COR24 BASIC — Demo Programs

A small gallery of programs that exercise the v1 interpreter. Each
demo has a matching `scripts/demo-<name>.sh` wrapper that builds (if
needed) and runs the corresponding `examples/<name>.bas` through
`pv24t`, the host-side p-code interpreter.

## Running

```sh
./scripts/build-basic.sh        # one-time, or after editing src/
./scripts/demo-hello.sh
./scripts/demo-calc.sh
./scripts/demo-fizzbuzz.sh
./scripts/demo-fibonacci.sh
./scripts/demo-factorial.sh
```

Each script feeds the `.bas` file to the interpreter as if it were
typed at the REPL. The files include trailing `RUN` and `BYE` lines
so they run end-to-end and exit cleanly.

## Demo Index

| Script | File | What it shows |
|---|---|---|
| `demo-hello.sh` | `examples/hello.bas` | Immediate-mode `PRINT` and `BYE`. The smallest possible program. |
| `demo-calc.sh` | `examples/calc.bas` | Arithmetic, `LET`, mixed string/expression `PRINT`. *(Note: shows the open ABS bug — see issue #1.)* |
| `demo-fizzbuzz.sh` | `examples/fizzbuzz.bas` | `FOR`/`NEXT`, `IF`...`THEN GOTO`, divisibility via `(I/N)*N=I`, multi-target dispatch. |
| `demo-fibonacci.sh` | `examples/fibonacci.bas` | Iterative two-variable swap inside `FOR`/`NEXT`. First 10 Fibonacci numbers. |
| `demo-factorial.sh` | `examples/factorial.bas` | Iterative product accumulator inside `FOR`/`NEXT`. Mixed-type `PRINT` of `N;"!=";F`. |
| `demo-count.sh` | `examples/count.bas` | Minimal `FOR`/`NEXT` smoke test, counts 1..10. |
| `demo-memdump.sh` | `examples/memdump.bas` | `POKE` then `PEEK` round-trip on a low scratch address. Demonstrates the byte-MMIO syntax. |
| *(no wrapper)* | `examples/blink.bas` | LED blink loop targeting the COR24 LED MMIO at `0xFF0000`. **Hardware/cor24-emu only** — `pv24t` traps because the address is outside its VM memory. |

## Demo Notes

### hello.bas

```basic
PRINT "HELLO WORLD"
BYE
```

Immediate mode — no line numbers. The simplest possible smoke test.

### calc.bas

Demonstrates arithmetic precedence (`(A+B)*2`), integer division
(`100/7=14`), and the mixed-type `PRINT` form (`PRINT "X=";X`).

> **Open bug:** the last line shows `ABS(-42)=10` instead of `42`.
> `ABS` is declared in `src/basic_tokens.pas` but not initialized in
> the `ik` keyword table, so the tokenizer falls through and consumes
> `A` as variable A. Tracked in
> [sw-cor24-basic#1](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic/issues/1)
> and queued as saga step `025-fix-abs-bug`.

### fizzbuzz.bas

There is no `MOD` operator in v1. Divisibility is tested with
`(I/N)*N = I` since integer division truncates. The dispatch chain
checks 15 first, then 3, then 5; each branch jumps to a print line
and back to `NEXT` via `GOTO`.

### fibonacci.bas

Classic three-variable rotation:

```basic
50 PRINT A
60 LET C=A+B
70 LET A=B
80 LET B=C
```

Outputs the first ten terms (0..34). Watch out for overflow: integers
are 24-bit signed, so the sequence stays valid up through the low
millions.

### factorial.bas

`N!` via straight `FOR I=1 TO N` accumulation. With `N=7` you get
`5040`. The 24-bit integer ceiling caps you at `12!` (479,001,600);
`13!` overflows.

### count.bas

The smallest stored-program example: `FOR I=1 TO 10 / PRINT I / NEXT`.
Useful as a baseline when poking at the build pipeline.

### memdump.bas

Pokes the bytes `72`, `73`, `33` ('H', 'I', '!') into addresses
`100..102`, then loops with `PEEK(I)` to print each byte:

```
100=72
101=73
102=33
```

The address range is just regular `pv24t` VM memory; no MMIO. The
demo exists to show that the language form `POKE addr,val` and
`PEEK(addr)` round-trips correctly. For real MMIO targets, see
`blink.bas`.

### blink.bas — hardware only

`POKE 16711680,0` then `POKE 16711680,1` toggles bit 0 at COR24's
`IO_LEDSWDAT` register (`0xFF0000`), where LED D2 lives. On real
hardware (or `cor24-emu`) this physically blinks the LED.

**Under `pv24t` it traps.** The host-side p-code interpreter only
allocates linear VM memory and `0xFF0000` is far outside the
allocated region. There is intentionally **no `demo-blink.sh`
wrapper** — running it would just trap. To exercise it, target the
emulator/hardware p-code VM (`pvm.s`) instead of `pv24t`. That path
isn't wired into `build-basic.sh` yet; it'll come with the unit
build pipeline (steps 018–019) which produces a `.p24m` image
loadable by `cor24-emu`.

Same caveat applies to `LED`/switch-style demos in general: under
`pv24t` the LED syscall (`sys 3`) is a no-op and `READ_SWITCH`
(`sys 6`) always returns 0.

## Recursion?

**Short answer:** no, not usefully.

COR24 BASIC v1 has `GOSUB`/`RETURN` with a 64-deep return stack, so
you *can* call a subroutine from within itself — the return addresses
nest correctly. But there are no parameters and no per-call locals:
all 26 variables (`A`..`Z`) are global. Each "recursive" call
overwrites the same storage as its caller, so by the time `RETURN`
fires, the supposed "local state" is gone.

There is also no tail-call optimization. `GOSUB` always pushes,
`RETURN` always pops; a `GOSUB` immediately followed by `RETURN`
still consumes a stack slot for the duration of the call. Every level
counts against the 64-slot ceiling.

The practical implication: write loops, not recursion. Factorial,
Fibonacci, GCD, and friends are all comfortable as iterative
`FOR`/`NEXT` or `GOTO` loops, which is exactly the style this
flavor of BASIC was designed for.

## Adding a new demo

1. Drop `examples/<name>.bas` ending with `RUN` and `BYE`.
2. Copy an existing wrapper:
   ```sh
   cp scripts/demo-hello.sh scripts/demo-<name>.sh
   sed -i '' "s/hello/<name>/g" scripts/demo-<name>.sh
   ```
3. Add a row to the table above.
4. `./scripts/demo-<name>.sh` to verify.

## Status

**v1 in progress** — interpreter is end-to-end functional under
`pv24t`. All v1 statements work: LET, PRINT (with column-aware
formatting), INPUT (with `?REDO`), IF...THEN, GOTO, GOSUB/RETURN,
FOR/TO/STEP/NEXT, REM, STOP, END, PEEK/POKE. Commands LIST, RUN,
NEW, BYE all work.

Pipeline: `.pas → p24p → .spc → pl24r → pa24r → .p24 → pv24t`

The whole interpreter currently lives in a single file
`src/basic.pas`. Splitting into p-code units (steps 018–019) is
queued and will lift the remaining single-file source ceiling.

Known limitations:
- Source ceiling: p24p input buffer is currently 16384 bytes
  (bumped from 8192 in sw-cor24-pascal#2). Step 018/019 will
  remove this entirely via the unit build.
- LED MMIO and switch input are no-ops under `pv24t`. Real
  hardware paths via `cor24-emu` aren't wired into the build yet.
- One known runtime bug: ABS not initialized in keyword table
  (sw-cor24-basic#1).

## Building and running

```sh
./scripts/build-basic.sh        # compile src/basic.pas to build/basic.p24
./scripts/run-basic.sh examples/hello.bas
```

## Demos

Six CLI demos under `scripts/demo-*.sh`. See [docs/demos.md](docs/demos.md)
for the full gallery.

```sh
./scripts/demo-hello.sh
./scripts/demo-calc.sh
./scripts/demo-fizzbuzz.sh
./scripts/demo-fibonacci.sh
./scripts/demo-factorial.sh
./scripts/demo-count.sh
./scripts/demo-memdump.sh
```

# sw-cor24-basic

COR24 BASIC v1 — a 1970s time-sharing BASIC interpreter for the COR24
p-code VM.

## Overview

COR24 BASIC is a line-numbered, integer-only BASIC interpreter inspired
by UNIVAC 1100-series terminal BASIC with teletype and paper-tape
workflows. It runs on the COR24 p-code virtual machine and emphasizes
simplicity, hardware access (PEEK/POKE), and interactive program
development over compatibility with later BASIC standards.

## Architecture

```
BASIC interpreter (Pascal) → p24p → .spc → pa24r → .p24 → pvm.s → COR24
```

The interpreter is a Pascal program compiled to p-code. It runs on the
p-code VM as a language layer:

- **Layer 0**: COR24 hardware / emulator
- **Layer 1**: P-code VM (language-neutral abstract machine)
- **Layer 2**: BASIC runtime (I/O, line storage, stacks, PEEK/POKE)
- **Layer 3**: BASIC interpreter (tokenizer, parser, dispatch, execution)

## Dialect: COR24 BASIC v1

- Line-numbered stored programs, tokenized on entry
- Signed 24-bit integer arithmetic (no floating point)
- Interactive: immediate mode + stored program mode (RUN)
- Terminal/teletype oriented
- Statements: LET, PRINT, INPUT, IF...THEN, GOTO, GOSUB, RETURN,
  FOR...TO...STEP, NEXT, STOP, END, REM
- Commands: LIST, RUN, NEW, BYE
- Functions: PEEK, POKE, ABS
- Variables: A-Z (26 scalar integers)

## Positioning

BASIC complements Pascal in the COR24 ecosystem:

| | Pascal | BASIC |
|---|--------|-------|
| Execution | Compiled to p-code | Interpreted on p-code VM |
| Style | Structured, typed | Dynamic, interactive |
| Use case | Application development | Hardware bring-up, scripting, monitor |

## Status

**In progress** — tokenizer implemented and tested.

Pipeline: `.pas → p24p → .spc → pl24r → pa24r → .p24 → pv24t`

- `src/basic_tokens.pas` — token definitions, keyword table, detokenizer
- `src/basic_lex.pas` — tokenizer (keywords, integers, strings, variables,
  operators, delimiters, REM, line numbers)

Known toolchain limitations:
- p24p UART input limited to ~3.5KB per compilation; source files must
  be compact (use `--stack-kilobytes 8` and `-u` flag)
- pvm.s globals too small for arrays (sw-cor24-pcode #1); using pv24t

## Dependencies

| Project | Repo | Role |
|---------|------|------|
| P-code VM | sw-cor24-pcode | Execution substrate |
| Pascal compiler | sw-cor24-pascal | Compiles BASIC interpreter |
| COR24 emulator | sw-cor24-emulator | Testing and running |

## Documentation

- [Product Requirements](docs/prd.md)
- [Architecture](docs/architecture.md)
- [Design](docs/design.md)
- [Implementation Plan](docs/plan.md)
- [Research Notes](docs/research.txt)

## License

MIT — see [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details.

Copyright (c) 2026 Michael A Wright

# sw-cor24-basic
